perturbation.original_text: None of the parties shall divulge to any person, (other than those whose provenance it is to know it, or with proper authority) or use any trade secrets or confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other.
perturbation.changed_text: None of the parties shall divulge to any person, or use any confidential information or any financial or trade information relating to the other, which they acquire as a result of the discussing of or the entering into agreements with each other. Trade secrets shall be defined at the discretion of each party.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'trade secrets shall be defined at the discretion of each party', the contract creates a contradiction, as this is not how trade secrets are protected under the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts NRS 600A.030, which provides a specific definition of 'trade secret'. Allowing each party to define 'trade secret' at their discretion conflicts with the statutory definition and weakens legal protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NRS: CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) [Rev. 6/29/2024 4:37:37 PM--2023] CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) NRS 600A.010 Short title. NRS 600A.030 Definitions. NRS 600A.032 Presumption of reasonable effort by owner to maintain secrecy; rebuttal of presumption. NRS 600A.035 Theft of trade secrets prohibited; criminal penalties. NRS 600A.040 Actual or threatened misappropriation: Injunctive relief. NRS 600A.050 Misappropriation: Damages. NRS 600A.055 Misappropriation and posting or dissemination on Internet: Effect. NRS 600A.060 Attorneys fees. NRS 600A.070 Preservation of secrecy. NRS 600A.080 Limitation of actions. NRS 600A.090 Effect of chapter on other law and remedies. NRS 600A.100 Inapplicability of chapter to acts which occurred before, or began before and continue after, July 1, 1987. _________ NRS 600A.010 Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added to NRS by 1987, 19 ) NRS 600A.030 Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. Improper means includes, without limitation: (a) Theft; (b) Bribery; (c) Misrepresentation; (d) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy; (e) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty imposed by common law, statute, contract, license, protective order or other court or administrative order; and (f) Espionage through electronic or other means. 2. Misappropriation means: (a) Acquisition of the trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (1) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (2) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had used improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the law because it allows each party to define 'trade secrets' at their own discretion, which directly conflicts with the statutory definition provided in NRS 600A.030. The scraped law snippet defines 'misappropriation' in terms of 'trade secret of another', implying a standard definition. Allowing each party to redefine the term undermines the legal protection intended by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties each undertake to the other that for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, they will not, without prior written consent of the other, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those Banks, Financial Institutions, government representatives, clients, investors, traders, associates, legal advisors and financial advisors with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties each undertake to the other that for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, they will not, without prior written consent of the other, directly or indirectly through third parties, make or seek to make contact or communication with those government representatives with whom they have been placed into contact with by the other or to whom the names, addresses and other pertinent information has been released to them by the other. This restriction does not apply if the government representative is considered a lobbyist.
perturbation.explanation: Lobbying is a regulated activity with its own set of rules. Excluding lobbyists from the non-circumvention clause could contradict laws regarding transparency and registration of lobbyists.
perturbation.law_explanation: By excluding lobbyists, the contract potentially circumvents regulations intended to ensure transparency in government interactions, contradicting the spirit and potentially the letter of NRS 218H regarding lobbying and its regulation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception for lobbyists from the non-circumvention clause, which contradicts the intent of regulations designed to ensure transparency in government interactions, as per the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Nevada Revised Statutes Chapter 218H, focuses on lobbying regulations, including registration and reporting requirements. By potentially allowing undisclosed contacts with lobbyists, the contract modification could be used to circumvent these regulations, thus creating a legal contradiction by undermining the purpose of these lobbying laws.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada, in accordance with the rules then in effect of the American Arbitration Association, The arbitrator(s) may grant injunctions or other relief in such dispute or controversy.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute or controversy arising out of or relating to any interpretation, construction, performance, or breach of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively by non-binding arbitration in Las Vegas, Nevada. The arbitrator's decision is purely advisory.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'binding arbitration' to 'non-binding arbitration' alters the enforceability of the arbitrator's decision. Binding arbitration is legally enforceable, whereas non-binding arbitration is not.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original agreement specified binding arbitration, enforceable under NRS 38.206. Changing it to non-binding contradicts the purpose of arbitration as a means of final dispute resolution, effectively rendering the arbitration clause meaningless under Nevada law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'binding arbitration' to 'non-binding arbitration' directly contradicts the legal intent of arbitration as a method of final dispute resolution. NRS 38 covers mediation and arbitration, and although the snippet does not explicitly define binding versus non-binding, the Law Explanation indicates that Nevada law (NRS 38.206) enforces binding arbitration agreements, implying that removing the 'binding' aspect undermines the agreement's enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that any breach of the terms and conditions of this Agreement by either party or its employees, agents, representatives or associates may render the seeking of liquidated damages, by the other and the cancellation and termination of all agreements and transactions.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that any breach of the terms and conditions of this Agreement by either party or its employees, agents, representatives or associates may render the seeking of liquidated damages, by the other. The amount of liquidated damages will be determined solely by the breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: Liquidated damages clauses must be reasonable and not punitive. Allowing the breaching party to determine the amount contradicts this principle.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104A.2718 outlines requirements for liquidated damages, including reasonableness. Granting the breaching party sole discretion over the amount undermines the principle of a fair and reasonable estimate of actual damages, contradicting Nevada law.

(Added to NRS by 1989, 340 ; A 1989, 721 ) ARTICLE 2A LEASES Part 1 General Provisions NRS 104A.2101 Short title. This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code—Leases. (Added to NRS by 1989, 340 ; A 1989, 721 ) NRS 104A.2102 Scope. 1. This article applies to any transaction, regardless of form, that creates a lease and, in the case of a hybrid lease, it applies to the extent provided in subsection 2. 2. In a hybrid lease: (a) If the lease-of-goods aspects do not predominate: (1) Only the provisions of this Article which relate primarily to the lease-of-goods aspects of the transaction apply, and the provisions that relate primarily to the transaction as a whole do not apply; (2) NRS 104A.2209 applies if the lease is a finance lease; and (3) NRS 104A.2407 applies to the promises of the lessee in a finance lease to the extent the promises are consideration for the right to possession and use of the leased goods. (b) If the lease-of-goods aspects predominate, this Article applies to the transaction, but this does not preclude application in appropriate circumstances of other law to the aspects of the lease which do not relate to the lease of goods. (Added to NRS by 1989, 340 ; A 1989, 721 ; 2023, 3239 ) NRS 104A.2103 Definitions and index of definitions. 1. In this Article unless the context otherwise requires: (a) Buyer i
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the breaching party to determine the amount of liquidated damages. This directly contradicts the principle that liquidated damages must be a reasonable estimate of actual damages, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'reasonableness' in the context of liquidated damages, the legal principle that liquidated damages cannot be punitive is a well-established legal concept. Giving the breaching party sole discretion inherently undermines the fairness and reasonableness required for such clauses to be enforceable, creating a legally questionable and potentially unenforceable contract term. The change introduces a clear contradiction of established legal principles by deviating from standard practices in liquidated damages assessment.
